raw: {"weights":{"R1.1":0.3,"R1.2":0.7,"R1.3":0.7,"R1.4":0.3,"R1.5":0.0,"R1.6":0.3,"R1.7":0.3,"RX.1":1.0,"RX.2":1.0,"RX.3":1.0,"RX.4":1.0,"RX.5":1.0},"active_set":["R1.2","R1.3","RX.1","RX.2","RX.3","RX.4","RX.5"],"rationale":{"R1.2":"The artifact contains a direct internal contradiction: it explicitly states FlexVAE 'makes no assumptions about the Lipschitz continuity of the generator network,' yet the convergence proof 'proceeds by bounding the gradient of the divergence term by a Lipschitz constant of the generator.' This self-refuting premise is a load-bearing logical failure under any reading of 'check this,' making R1.2 a clear central responsibility.","R1.3":"The empirical claims (8% over FactorVAE, 12% over beta-TCVAE) are anchored to 'the disentanglement metric proposed by Locatello et al.'—but Locatello et al. (2019) proposed several metrics (MIG, DCI, SAP, Factor, IRS). The singular definite article implies one canonical metric that does not exist, making evidence-claim alignment a central expected responsibility under a plausible interpretation of 'check.'"},"clarification_needed":true,"clarification_question":"Should I focus primarily on internal logical consistency (e.g., whether the proof's reliance on a Lipschitz constant contradicts the paper's stated assumption-free framing), or on verifying that the empirical claims are adequately grounded in the experimental evidence and citations as presented?"}

rationale.R1.2: The artifact contains a direct internal contradiction: it explicitly states FlexVAE 'makes no assumptions about the Lipschitz continuity of the generator network,' yet the convergence proof 'proceeds by bounding the gradient of the divergence term by a Lipschitz constant of the generator.' This self-refuting premise is a load-bearing logical failure under any reading of 'check this,' making R1.2 a clear central responsibility.
rationale.R1.3: The empirical claims (8% over FactorVAE, 12% over beta-TCVAE) are anchored to 'the disentanglement metric proposed by Locatello et al.'—but Locatello et al. (2019) proposed several metrics (MIG, DCI, SAP, Factor, IRS). The singular definite article implies one canonical metric that does not exist, making evidence-claim alignment a central expected responsibility under a plausible interpretation of 'check.'

parsed.clarification_question: Should I focus primarily on internal logical consistency (e.g., whether the proof's reliance on a Lipschitz constant contradicts the paper's stated assumption-free framing), or on verifying that the empirical claims are adequately grounded in the experimental evidence and citations as presented?
